Name: spectral_latency_optimizer
Title: Spectral Latency Optimization for Training-Free Machine Learning
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks can be leveraged to develop a training-free machine learning framework that optimizes latency-critical inference, by dynamically allocating computational resources based on real-time activation geometry and spectral decay rates.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent work in training-free machine learning, such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation, but differs in its focus on latency-critical inference and the use of spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks. Unlike existing methods, our approach aims to provide a rigorous, mathematically transparent framework for optimizing latency and computational resources in machine learning models.
Abstract: We propose a novel training-free machine learning framework that leverages spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks to optimize latency-critical inference. Our approach dynamically allocates computational resources based on real-time activation geometry and spectral decay rates, ensuring strict latency budgets and improving model robustness. We theoretically ground our framework in information-theoretic bounds and demonstrate its effectiveness on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, achieving a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier. Our work provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed framework on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, including WILDS and ICL benchmarks.
Compare the performance of our approach with existing training-free machine learning methods, such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation.
Investigate the effect of spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks on model robustness and computational efficiency.
Develop a dynamic early-exit strategy based on principal component variance thresholds and compute allocation.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework may require significant computational resources for spectral decomposition and PAC-Bayes bound computation.
The approach may not be suitable for very large models or datasets due to computational constraints.
Theoretical guarantees may not always translate to empirical performance improvements.
